request.question: In which year was the album released which included the single "Long Kiss Goodnight", which was used as a sample in the song "Rap name"?

title: Sons of the P
Rap Name
Disneyland Forever
Yesterday (Tyler Shaw album)
Tyler Shaw
Let Me Rock You
Charli Baltimore
Michael Licari
Life After Death
Kiss Goodnight
sentences: Sons of the P is the second album by American rap group Digital Underground, released on October 15, 1991.

The album featured two hit singles, "No Nose Job" and "Kiss You Back", both of which were written by, and featured the lead vocals of Greg Jacobs (a.k.a. Shock G/Humpty Hump), and the latter of which featured multi-layered choruses and background vocals sung by Boni Boyer, who briefly worked with D.U. shortly after her stint with Prince's Sign of the Times/Love Sexy band.

It is sometimes mistakenly reported that Kiss You Back was co-written & co-performed by George Clinton, but his name appropriately appears in the writers credit due to a sample of "(Not Just) Knee Deep" by Funkadelic.

He did however actively participate in the writing and recording of the title track "Sons of the P", to which he also contributed vocals, and which marked one of the earliest studio guest appearances by Clinton on a Hip Hop release, preceded only by Kurtis Blow's "Magilla Gorilla" released in 1986.
Both the album and the single "Kiss You Back" were each certified Gold by the RIAA.
"Rap Name" is the debut single of American rapper Obie Trice, released as a limited edition vinyl recording, taken from the deluxe edition of the soundtrack to the film "8 Mile".
The song features vocals from fellow-label mate Eminem, who sampled the track in two tracks for his album "The Eminem Show", released earlier in 2002.
These tracks are "Drips" and the intro of the album's lead single, "Without Me", as well as a few other lines from the song.
The song also features a vocal sample of The Notorious B.I.G. from his song "Long Kiss Goodnight" from the album "Life After Death".
The official video was filmed in Detroit with featured cameo appearances by Eminem's group D12.
Disneyland Forever was a nighttime spectacular at Disneyland that premiered alongside the "Paint the Night" parade and "World of Color—Celebrate!
" on May 21, 2015 as part of the park's 60th anniversary celebration.
The show was produced by Walt Disney Creative Entertainment, under direction of Steve Davison.
"Disneyland Forever" incorporates fireworks, projection mapping, fire, lasers, and searchlights to depict scenes from Disney films.
The show closed on September 5th, 2016 as the Diamond Celebration concluded along with World of Color Celebrate.
The shows exit song "A Kiss Goodnight" would be later be released on CD with a book bearing the songs name.
Yesterday is the debut studio album recorded by Canadian singer-songwriter Tyler Shaw, released September 4, 2015 through Tyler Ip, a label imprint of Sony Music Canada.
It was preceded by the release of four singles between 2012 and 2015, including the Gold-certified lead single, "Kiss Goodnight".
Shaw co-wrote twelve of the thirteen songs, including all four of the aforementioned singles.
Tyler Shaw (born April 8, 1993) is a Canadian singer and the winner of the "MuchMusic Coca-Cola Covers Contest 2012" organized by the specialized Canadian music cable channel Much (TV channel).
He rose to fame through the contest and subsequently released his debut single, "Kiss Goodnight", through Sony Music Canada in December 2012.
The song was certified Gold by Music Canada in April 2013.
Shaw's debut studio album, "Yesterday", was released on September 4, 2015.
Let Me Rock You is the third album released by former Kiss drummer Peter Criss.
Due to poor sales for his previous album, "Out of Control", "Let Me Rock You" was not released in the United States until 1998, when it was reissued on CD.
The album was produced by Vini Poncia, who previously produced Criss's 1978 solo album (officially released as a Kiss album).
"Let Me Rock You" features the song "Feels Like Heaven", written by Criss's former bandmate, Gene Simmons.
Vinnie Cusano also co-wrote a song for the album; at the same year the album was released, Cusano became known as Vinnie Vincent when he replaced Kiss guitarist Ace Frehley.
The album cover features Peter Criss for the first time without his Kiss makeup, as he didn't appear on the cover of his solo album "Out of Control".
One year later, Kiss also decided to take off their makeup for their "Lick It Up" album.
Tiffany Lane (born August 16, 1974), better known as Charli Baltimore, is an American rapper–songwriter.
Her stage name is taken from Geena Davis's character in the film "The Long Kiss Goodnight".
Michael Licari is an American television composer and songwriter.
He has worked as a composer on the television series, "Deliver Me", documentary "Triple the Triplets" (2005).

He has also worked as a song writer and performer on the 2007 film "Charlie Bartlett" (for the song "Nice Vibes"), and on the 1996 film "The Long Kiss Goodnight", as an arranger (for the "Jingle Bells Marching Band").
He has also worked on award-winning drama series, "The Guiding Light", "Another World","As The World Turns", "Sunset Beach", and "Santa Barbara".
Life After Death is the second and final studio album by American rapper The Notorious B.I.G., released on March 25, 1997, on Bad Boy Records and Arista Records.
A double album, it was released posthumously following his death on March 9, 1997.
It features collaborations with guest artists such as 112, Jay-Z, Lil' Kim, Mase, Bone Thugs-n-Harmony, Too $hort, Angela Winbush, D.M.C. of Run-D.M.C.
, R. Kelly, The LOX and Puff Daddy.
"Life After Death" exhibits The Notorious B.I.G. further delving into the mafioso rap subgenre.
The album is a sequel to his first album, "Ready to Die", and picks up where the last song, "Suicidal Thoughts", ends.
"Kiss Goodnight" is the debut single released by Canadian singer-songwriter Tyler Shaw.
The song was written by Shaw in conjunction with Stephen Kozmeniuk and Todd Clark, and was produced by Kozmeniuk and Zubin Thakkar.

It was released December 4, 2012 through Sony Music Canada as Shaw's first official single following his win of the second CocaCola MuchMusic Covers Contest (2012) and serves as the lead single for Shaw's debut studio album, "Yesterday" (2015).
A franglais version featuring vocals by french pop artist Nathalie Noël, entitled "Je veux juste rester là" ("I Just Want to Stay There"), was released April 9, 2013.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Agent role: Produce one compact second-hop retrieval query targeting the missing bridge or supporting evidence.

Pipeline position: after summary1 and before second-hop retrieval. Inputs are the original question and the first-hop intermediate state. Functional responsibility: produce one focused retrieval query that is likely to recover the missing evidence needed by the downstream agents. Query quality is determined by evidence recovery, not by whether the query itself resembles or contains the final answer.

Native output contract: The module must consume `question` and `summary_1` and produce exactly one compact second-hop query field named `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include every distinct entity token visible in the inputs in the single terse query and place at most one neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the targeted relation or attribute (for example: "EntityA EntityB director").
- If a visible disambiguator or attribute token appears adjacent to an entity in the inputs (for example a multiword service name, an explicit page title, a year, or an attribute marker like a birth/producer/service/series cue), include that disambiguator token together with the associated entity in the query rather than dropping or truncating it.
- If `summary_1` contains an explicit page-title token sequence (for example an instruction-like ExactPageTitle or Fetch page title), prefer that exact page-title token sequence as the query value.
- When the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise plain-text summary string (no extra fields, labels, markup, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification.
- Primary goal: explicitly integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the supported linkage it needs.

Explicit bridging requirement:
- When the passages supply a clear linking fact between the context entity and information in the passages, produce a concise 1–2 sentence integrated summary that: (1) names the context entity using the exact form present in `context`, (2) names the supporting entity or attribute using the exact form present in `passages`, and (3) states the explicit relation (membership, creator attribution, parentage, partnership, award, representation, founding, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Phrase this as one short bridging sentence when possible.
- For relations that naturally involve two distinct facts (for example, parentage plus representation, or a person linked to a show and also to an occupation), it is acceptable to use two short sentences within the same single-string summary: e.g., one sentence for parentage and one for representation. Still observe the 1–2 sentence limit.

Inclusion of passage-supplied disambiguators (only when directly relevant):
- If a passage supplies canonical multi-token names or other disambiguating attributes needed to resolve the question, include them inline using the wording from the passages: append birthdates in parentheses immediately after the associated full canonical name (e.g., Name (YYYY–) ), state group type and formation year using the passage's phrasing (e.g., "a [group type] formed in [year]"), and include a canonical member list exactly as shown (comma-separated) only when the passage presents a definitive membership list. Do not add ancillary attributes that are not necessary to the identification or explicit linking required by the question.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (e.g., "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages, comma-separated if multiple. Do not add explanatory clauses, relations, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (for example: "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Preserve uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 short sentences). Do not expand into biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Use only facts and phrasing present in `context` and `passages`; do not introduce or infer unsupported information.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.
- Do not assert that a passage's member list is complete unless the passage explicitly states it is definitive.

Format enforcement:
- Return a single plain-text string only. Do not emit JSON, dictionaries, labels, bullets, or any structured artifact—only the concise natural-language summary string.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else. Before emitting that string, you may apply only the narrowly permitted normalizations listed below. After applying permitted normalizations, the emitted string must equal the minimal label text from the prioritized summary verbatim (see normalization constraints). Specific allowed single-token answers: for a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no. For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces), subject only to the permitted normalizations. Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if a permitted normalization applies (see below).

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) and the permitted normalizations as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Permitted normalizations and extraction rules (apply only when the token(s) to emit appear verbatim in the prioritized summary and only to produce a strictly smaller, but still verbatim-supported, minimal label):
- Parenthetical/core-name stripping: if the candidate label in the prioritized summary includes common corporate suffixes or parenthetical abbreviations (for example variants like "Inc.", "LLC", or parenthetical acronyms), strip those suffixes/parentheticals and emit the core name only (do not invent or substitute other words).
- Hyphenated composite phrase extraction: if the prioritized summary presents a composite genre or label in the form "<modifier> X-Y" (for example an extra leading modifier followed by a hyphenated core like "classic action-adventure"), emit only the hyphenated core token exactly as shown ("X-Y").
- Trailing generic-descriptor removal: if the candidate minimal answer appears as a proper-name followed by a single trailing generic descriptor token of the form "<ProperName> crisis" (or an equivalent single-word generic classifier), strip the trailing generic token and emit only the proper-name sequence.
- Denomination-occupation reduction: if the question's scope requests a denomination/kind and the prioritized summary contains the form "<denomination> priest" (or equivalent denomination + occupational noun), emit only the denomination token(s) (i.e., the minimal denomination label).

Normalization constraints:
- Do not apply normalizations unless the text to emit appears verbatim in the prioritized summary (summary_2, or summary_1 only if summary_2 is silent for the answer).
- Normalizations may only remove or trim tokens; they must not add, invent, or substitute new content.
- Do not globally strip generic classifier nouns from multi-token geographic names or other proper nouns unless covered by the permitted rules above.

Tie-break and variant-selection rules:
- If a role-descriptor and a proper name co-occur in the prioritized summary in the form "<role> <Name>", prefer emitting the named individual exactly as shown (emit the Name only) unless the prioritized summary presents the role as the requested concise label explicitly without a name.
- For title or label-variant conflicts, prefer an alternate or parenthetical variant only when the prioritized summary explicitly marks that variant as an alias, alternate name, or the original-language title (or otherwise explicitly labels it as a variant) AND that variant matches the question's scope. If no such explicit labeling appears, emit the primary label supported by summary_2.

Strict emission rule (verbatim-emission):
- The final `answer` must equal the minimal label text from the prioritized summary verbatim after applying only the permitted normalizations above. Do not append or prepend occupational nouns, role descriptors, location qualifiers, or explanatory tokens that are not part of that verbatim label.

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.question: In which year was the album released which included the single "Long Kiss Goodnight", which was used as a sample in the song "Rap name"?

Disneyland Forever
Yesterday (Tyler Shaw album)

response.hop1_query: In which year was the album released which included the single "Long Kiss Goodnight", which was used as a sample in the song "Rap name"?
hop1_docs: Rap Name | "Rap Name" is the debut single of American rapper Obie Trice, released as a limited edition vinyl recording, taken from the deluxe edition of the soundtrack to the film "8 Mile".  The song features vocals from fellow-label mate Eminem, who sampled the track in two tracks for his album "The Eminem Show", released earlier in 2002.  These tracks are "Drips" and the intro of the album's lead single, "Without Me", as well as a few other lines from the song.  The song also features a vocal sample of The Notorious B.I.G. from his song "Long Kiss Goodnight" from the album "Life After Death".  The official video was filmed in Detroit with featured cameo appearances by Eminem's group D12.

Church (song) | "Church" is a song by rapper T-Pain.  It is a fast-paced song led by guitar riffs and is one of the songs in which T-Pain raps.  It appears on his album "Epiphany" and was released as the third single.  The song "features" Teddy Verseti, one of T-Pain's aliases, which he uses when rapping vigorously.  The song is about wanting to fight someone following a confrontation at a club.  It was confirmed as the third single from "Epiphany" in an interview promoting the album.  It contains a sample of the song "Which Way to America" by Living Colour.

The Game (Alyssa Reid song) | "The Game" is a song by "The Next Star" contestant Alyssa Reid from her 2011 album "The Game".  It is the title track of the album and the album's second single, as well as Alyssa's second most famous single, beat out by her previous single "Alone Again", officially released May 31st, 2011.  It was preceded by the album's lead single, "Alone Again", featuring P. Reign, which heavily sampled the 1987 Heart song "Alone", except included new verses and rap.  "The Game" samples parts of the new verses of "Alone Again".

Goodnight Kisses | "Goodnight Kisses" is a song by American R&B singer Charlie Wilson.  It was released on October 7, 2014.  The song is the first single from the seventh studio album "Forever Charlie" (2015).

Sons of the P | Sons of the P is the second album by American rap group Digital Underground, released on October 15, 1991.  The album featured two hit singles, "No Nose Job" and "Kiss You Back", both of which were written by, and featured the lead vocals of Greg Jacobs (a.k.a. Shock G/Humpty Hump), and the latter of which featured multi-layered choruses and background vocals sung by Boni Boyer, who briefly worked with D.U. shortly after her stint with Prince's Sign of the Times/Love Sexy band.  It is sometimes mistakenly reported that Kiss You Back was co-written & co-performed by George Clinton, but his name appropriately appears in the writers credit due to a sample of "(Not Just) Knee Deep" by Funkadelic.  He did however actively participate in the writing and recording of the title track "Sons of the P", to which he also contributed vocals, and which marked one of the earliest studio guest appearances by Clinton on a Hip Hop release, preceded only by Kurtis Blow's "Magilla Gorilla" released in 1986.  Both the album and the single "Kiss You Back" were each certified Gold by the RIAA.

Fatherhood (album) | Fatherhood was the third of five albums of home-made recordings, released by Stephen Jones under the name Baby Bird in 1995.  The album includes a version of the song "Goodnight", which was subsequently released as the first single by Jones' band Babybird (written as one word).

Kiss You Back | "Kiss You Back" was the lead single released from Digital Underground's second studio album (their third official release), "Sons of the P".  The song sampled Funkadelic's 1979 hit, "(Not Just) Knee Deep" for which the song's writers, George Clinton and Philippé Wynne, received writing credits.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- "Rap Name" — [1]: '"Rap Name" is the debut single'
- Obie Trice — [1]: 'American rapper Obie Trice'
- The Eminem Show — [1]: 'album "The Eminem Show", released earlier in 2002.'
- The Notorious B.I.G. — [1]: 'vocal sample of The Notorious B.I.G.'
- "Long Kiss Goodnight" — [1]: '"Long Kiss Goodnight" from the album "Life After Death"'
- "Life After Death" (album) — [1]: '"Life After Death"'

Ambiguities:
- None in the passages: "Life After Death" is referenced as an album and tied to The Notorious B.I.G. only in these passages.

Bridge / missing fact:
- The release year of the album "Life After Death" (the album that contains "Long Kiss Goodnight") is not given in the provided passages.

Retrieval direction:

response.hop2_query: Life After Death Notorious BIG release year
hop2_docs: Life After Death | Life After Death is the second and final studio album by American rapper The Notorious B.I.G., released on March 25, 1997, on Bad Boy Records and Arista Records.  A double album, it was released posthumously following his death on March 9, 1997.  It features collaborations with guest artists such as 112, Jay-Z, Lil' Kim, Mase, Bone Thugs-n-Harmony, Too $hort, Angela Winbush, D.M.C. of Run-D.M.C. , R. Kelly, The LOX and Puff Daddy.  "Life After Death" exhibits The Notorious B.I.G. further delving into the mafioso rap subgenre.  The album is a sequel to his first album, "Ready to Die", and picks up where the last song, "Suicidal Thoughts", ends.

Thug Life | Thug Life was an American hip hop group that consisted of Tupac Shakur, Big Syke, Stretch, Mopreme Shakur, The Rated R, and Macadoshis.  They released one album, 1994's "", before disbanding after Tupac died on September 13, 1996.  Big Syke died on December 5, 2016, 20 years after Shakur's death.

Hypnotize (The Notorious B.I.G. song) | "Hypnotize" is a single by American rapper The Notorious B.I.G., released as the first single from his album "Life After Death" on March 1, 1997 and the last song released in his lifetime, as he was killed in a drive-by shooting a week later.  It was the fifth song by a credited artist to hit number one posthumously.  It was ranked at number 30 on "Rolling Stone"' s 50 greatest rap songs of all time in hip hop history.

Sky's the Limit (The Notorious B.I.G. song) | "Sky's the Limit" is the third single from The Notorious B.I.G.'s second album "Life After Death".  It features vocals from R&B group 112 and somber production from Clark Kent.  It contains a sample from the songs "Is This Love" by Whitesnake, "My Flame" by Bobby Caldwell and "Keep On" by D. Train.  In the US, it was released as a triple A-side along with "Going Back to Cali" and "Kick In the Door".  The track was certified Gold by the RIAA in 1998.

Nasty Girl (The Notorious B.I.G. song) | "Nasty Girl" is a song by rapper The Notorious B.I.G.  It was released in 2005 in the US and on January 16, 2006 in the UK.  The single reached #1 in the United Kingdom (this being his first #1 in the country, just under a year after "rival" rapper 2Pac had also achieved his first #1 there also with "Ghetto Gospel").  The song features guest appearances from Jagged Edge, P. Diddy, Avery Storm, and Nelly and the video also contains guest appearances from Pharrell, Usher, Fat Joe, 8 Ball & MJG, Teairra Mari, Jazze Pha, DJ Green Lantern, Naomi Campbell and Memphis Bleek.  It can be found on the album "", a remixed album of Biggie Smalls' work.  The lyrical section rapped by Notorious B.I.G is actually lifted from another of his songs called "Nasty Boy", featured on his second album "Life After Death".  Despite this, the production to the song "Nasty Boy" is completely different from that for "Nasty Girl", and apart from the lyrical sample, and the second verse (rapped by P. Diddy) rapped in the style of Biggie's second verse of Nasty Boy, the two songs bear no similarities.  The chorus, sung by Jagged Edge, which has the line "Grab your titties for B.I.G.", references "Player's Anthem", which he says "Bitches, rub your titties if you love Big Poppa".

Mo Money Mo Problems | "Mo Money Mo Problems" is a single by The Notorious B.I.G., the second single from his album "Life After Death".  Released posthumously, the single topped the "Billboard" Hot 100 for two weeks in 1997, replacing "I'll Be Missing You" from the chart, Puff Daddy's tribute to the rapper's death himself.  The song is Notorious B.I.G.'s second posthumous number one single, following "Hypnotize", making him the only artist in Hot 100 history to have two #1 singles posthumously.  It was the sixth song to hit #1 posthumously for a credited artist.  The song was nominated for the 1998 Grammy Award for Best Rap Performance by a Duo or Group.

Life or Death (C-Murder album) | Life or Death is the debut album by American rapper C-Murder by No Limit Records on March 17, 1998, It was produced by Beats By the Pound.  Like most of No Limit's albums in the 1990s, the album was a success, peaking at #3 on the "Billboard" 200 and #1 on the Top R&B/Hip-Hop Albums, as well as #21 on the Top Heatseekers.  After debuting on the charts with 197,000 copies sold its first week out, "Life or Death" quickly achieved Platinum status moving over 1 million copies.  It is possible that the release date of this album started some tension between No Limit and Cash Money Records because Big Tymers released their debut How You Luv That on the same day.
hop2_titles: Sky's the Limit (The Notorious B.I.G. song)
response.summary_2: "Rap Name" uses a sample of "Long Kiss Goodnight" from the album "Life After Death", and "Life After Death" was released in 1997.
